nodes.name: Wines & Wine Regions
nodes.description: Wine regions, grape varietals, and classification systems

items.prompt: Which French region, northeast of Paris, produces the only wine legally allowed to be called Champagne, made via the traditional method of secondary fermentation in the bottle?
items.explanation:
Legally protected AOC region in northeastern France; the name cannot be used for sparkling wine produced elsewhere in the EU
Méthode champenoise (traditional method): secondary fermentation occurs inside the sealed bottle, producing natural CO2 bubbles
Primary grapes: Chardonnay (blanc de blancs), Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier
Non-vintage (NV) Champagne blends multiple years; vintage Champagne declares a single harvest year
Houses like Moët & Chandon, Veuve Clicquot, and Krug are headquartered in Épernay and Reims
Dom Pérignon, a Benedictine monk, is credited in legend with perfecting sparkling Champagne around 1697 — though the process was likely already known in England
Rosé Champagne can be made by blending still red wine into the base, or by brief skin contact

items.prompt: Which French wine region, divided by the Gironde estuary into a Left Bank (Cabernet Sauvignon-dominant) and a Right Bank (Merlot-dominant), is home to châteaux classified in the famous 1855 system?
items.explanation:
Located in southwestern France at the mouth of the Garonne and Dordogne rivers
Left Bank appellations: Médoc, Pauillac, Saint-Estèphe — Cabernet Sauvignon dominant, gravelly soils
Right Bank appellations: Saint-Émilion, Pomerol — Merlot dominant, clay and limestone soils
Bordeaux-style blends always mix multiple grapes; single-varietal Bordeaux is rare
Petrus (Pomerol, Right Bank) is among the world's most expensive wines without being in the 1855 classification
The city of Bordeaux has been a major wine trading hub since Roman times
Cold, wet vintages can devastate harvests; 1982 and 2000 are considered legendary years

items.prompt: Which French region, known as Bourgogne in French, produces Pinot Noir reds and Chardonnay whites, organized into a hierarchy of village, premier cru, and grand cru appellations?
items.explanation:
Located in east-central France; considered by many the world's greatest terroir for Pinot Noir and Chardonnay
Key sub-regions: Chablis (northern, steely Chardonnay), Côte de Nuits (reds), Côte de Beaune (whites and reds), Mâconnais
Unlike Bordeaux, most Burgundy wines are single-varietal: red = Pinot Noir, white = Chardonnay
Romanée-Conti, in Vosne-Romanée, is the world's most expensive wine per bottle
Burgundy's system of small vineyard ownership (climat) is a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2015
Napoleon's armies are said to have marched through Burgundy and drunk everything they found
Grand cru wines represent less than 2% of total Burgundy production

items.prompt: Which DOCG wine from the Langhe hills of Piedmont, Italy, made exclusively from Nebbiolo grapes and aged at least 38 months, is nicknamed the 'wine of kings, king of wines'?
items.explanation:
DOCG (highest Italian classification) from the Cuneo province of Piedmont, northwest Italy
Made from 100% Nebbiolo, one of Italy's most tannic and age-worthy grapes
Minimum aging: 38 months total, at least 18 months in oak; riserva requires 62 months
Favored by the House of Savoy and Turin's nobility in the 19th century — origin of the royal nickname
Paired with Barbaresco (also Nebbiolo, Piedmont) as the 'king and queen of Italian wines'
Aromas: roses, tar, leather, cherries, violets — distinctive combination from Nebbiolo
Produced in 11 communes including Barolo, La Morra, Castiglione Falletto, and Serralunga d'Alba

items.prompt: Which Tuscan Italian wine, made primarily from Sangiovese grapes, comes in the recognizable fiasco (straw-covered bottle) and is produced in the historic zone between Florence and Siena?
items.explanation:
DOC/DOCG wine from Tuscany, central Italy; Chianti Classico is the historic heartland between Florence and Siena
Minimum 70–80% Sangiovese; other permitted varieties include Canaiolo, Colorino, and up to 20% international grapes
The iconic fiasco (straw-covered flask) bottle is largely nostalgic; modern Chianti uses standard Bordeaux bottles
Chianti Classico earned DOCG status in 1984; marked with the Gallo Nero (Black Rooster) symbol
Gran Selezione tier (introduced 2014) represents single-vineyard, top-quality Chianti Classico
Sangiovese means 'blood of Jupiter' in Italian
Baron Ricasoli codified the Chianti blend in 1872; later became Italy's second prime minister

items.prompt: Which Spanish wine region, located along the Ebro River in northern Spain, produces Tempranillo-based reds classified by aging time as crianza, reserva, or gran reserva?
items.explanation:
Spain's most internationally recognized wine region; DOCa (Denominación de Origen Calificada) since 1991
Primary grape: Tempranillo, often blended with Garnacha, Mazuelo, and Graciano
Aging classifications: crianza (2 years, 1 in oak), reserva (3 years, 1 in oak), gran reserva (5 years, 2 in oak)
Three sub-regions: Rioja Alta (cooler, elegant), Rioja Alavesa (Basque Country, limestone), Rioja Oriental (hotter, fuller wines)
American oak barrels traditionally used, imparting vanilla and coconut notes — now also French oak
Phylloxera devastated France's vineyards in the 1870s; Bordeaux winemakers fled to Rioja and modernized its techniques
Rioja produces about 90% red wine; whites are mostly Viura (Macabeo) grape

items.prompt: Which California wine valley, famous for world-class Cabernet Sauvignon, shocked the wine world when its wines beat top French bottles in the 1976 blind tasting organized by Steven Spurrier in Paris?
items.explanation:
Located in Napa County, California, about 50 miles northeast of San Francisco
The 1976 Judgment of Paris (organized by Steven Spurrier): Napa wines topped both the red and white categories in blind tasting by French judges
Stag's Leap Wine Cellars 1973 Cabernet won the red category; Chateau Montelena 1973 Chardonnay won the white
AVA (American Viticultural Area) status since 1981; produces some of the world's most expensive Cabernets (Screaming Eagle, Opus One)
Napa Valley wine tourism contributes billions annually to the California economy
Over 400 wineries operate in a valley roughly 30 miles long and 5 miles wide
The valley's diverse soils and microclimates support over 40 grape varieties

items.prompt: Which aromatic white grape variety, native to the Rhine region of Germany, produces wines ranging from bone-dry to lusciously sweet and is known for high acidity, longevity, and notes of petrol when aged?
items.explanation:
Native to the Rhine Valley; major regions include Mosel, Rheingau, and Alsace (France)
Ranges from trocken (dry) to Trockenbeerenauslese (TBA), one of the world's sweetest and rarest wines
Predikats system (Kabinett → Spätlese → Auslese → Beerenauslese → Eiswein → TBA) denotes ripeness at harvest
The 'petrol' note (kerosene aroma) from TDN compound is considered a positive indicator of aging in quality Riesling
Alsace Riesling is typically drier and fuller-bodied than German counterparts; made from the same grape but different style
Germany's Mosel produces Riesling on steep slate slopes above the river; slate retains heat and gives mineral character
Riesling grapes are naturally high in acidity, making them naturally resistant to disease and well-suited for late harvest

items.prompt: Which red grape variety, originally from Cahors in southwest France, became the signature grape of Argentina's Mendoza region and now dominates Argentine wine exports?
items.explanation:
Originally a minor blending grape in Bordeaux and the dominant red of Cahors (Côt or Auxerrois in France)
Brought to Argentina in 1853 by French agronomist Michel Pouget, at the request of Domingo Sarmiento
Argentina's Mendoza, at high altitude (900–1,500 meters), produces intense, fruit-forward Malbecs with softer tannins than French versions
High altitude = more UV radiation, thicker grape skins = deeper color and more tannins
Oval dark-blue grapes with a 'plum, violet, and dark chocolate' flavor profile in New World style
Argentina now produces far more Malbec than France; the grape nearly disappeared from French vineyards after phylloxera
Argentine Malbec boom began in the 1990s; by 2000s it was the country's flagship export wine

items.prompt: Which Italian sparkling wine, made from at least 85% Glera grapes in the Veneto region, is produced by the Charmat method (tank fermentation) and was granted DOCG status in 2009?
items.explanation:
Produced in Veneto and Friuli-Venezia Giulia, northeastern Italy; name derives from the village of Prosecco near Trieste
Glera grape (formerly called Prosecco) must constitute at least 85% of the blend; in 2009, the name 'Prosecco' was restricted to the wine, not the grape
Charmat method: secondary fermentation in pressurized tanks (autoclave), not individual bottles — faster and cheaper than Champagne method
Top DOCG appellations: Conegliano Valdobbiadene Prosecco Superiore DOCG and Asolo Prosecco DOCG
Lighter, fruitier, and less yeasty than Champagne; flavors of peach, cream, and pear
Prosecco is the base of the Bellini cocktail (white peach purée + Prosecco, invented at Harry's Bar, Venice, 1948)
Italy exports over 600 million bottles of Prosecco annually; it outsells Champagne by volume worldwide

items.prompt: Which crisp, herbaceous white grape variety put New Zealand's Marlborough region on the global wine map and is also a key variety in France's Loire Valley appellations of Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé?
items.explanation:
Native to the Loire Valley of France; name may derive from sauvage (wild) + blanc (white)
Loire Valley: Sancerre and Pouilly-Fumé are benchmark appellations — flinty, mineral style
New Zealand's Marlborough region (South Island) produces the world's most recognized style: intensely aromatic with passionfruit, cut grass, and grapefruit
Cloudy Bay (1985 vintage) is credited with launching Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc onto the world stage
In Bordeaux, blended with Sémillon for both dry whites and sweet Sauternes
Fermentation in stainless steel (not oak) preserves the grape's fresh aromatic profile
Sauvignon Blanc is one of the parents (with Cabernet Franc) of Cabernet Sauvignon

items.prompt: Which fortified wine from Portugal's Douro Valley is made by adding grape brandy mid-fermentation to halt the process, preserving residual sugar, and is aged in lodges in Vila Nova de Gaia?
items.explanation:
Produced in the Douro Valley, northern Portugal; named after the city of Porto (Oporto)
Fortification: neutral grape brandy (aguardente) added during fermentation, killing yeast and leaving residual sugar at 18–20% ABV
Styles: ruby (young, fruity), tawny (barrel-aged, nutty), LBV (Late Bottled Vintage), Vintage (single year, rare)
Vintage Port is declared only in exceptional years and can age 20–50+ years
Traditionally aged in lodges (warehouses) in Vila Nova de Gaia, across the river from Porto
The Douro Valley's terraced schist vineyards are a UNESCO World Heritage Site
The Methuen Treaty (1703) between Britain and Portugal established favorable trade terms that drove Port's popularity in Britain

items.prompt: Which Spanish fortified wine, produced in the 'sherry triangle' of Jerez, Sanlúcar, and El Puerto de Santa María in Andalusia, ranges from bone-dry Fino to sweet Pedro Ximénez?
items.explanation:
DO Jerez-Xérès-Sherry, Andalusia, southern Spain; 'Sherry' is the English corruption of Jerez
Primary grape: Palomino (for Fino, Manzanilla, Amontillado, Oloroso); Pedro Ximénez (PX) for intensely sweet styles
Fino and Manzanilla: fortified to ~15% ABV, protected by a layer of flor (yeast) that prevents oxidation and imparts briny character
Oloroso: oxidatively aged, no flor, ~18% ABV, nutty and richer
The solera aging system: blending older and newer wines across barrels in tiers, ensuring consistency across vintages
Sherry was the most popular wine in Elizabethan England; Shakespeare's Falstaff praises 'sack' (an old name for Sherry) repeatedly
Harvey's Bristol Cream is the world's best-selling Sherry brand

items.prompt: Which gray-skinned grape, known as Pinot Grigio in Italy and Pinot Gris in Alsace, produces light, crisp wines in the Veneto style and rich, spicy, full-bodied wines in the Alsatian style?
items.explanation:
The same gray-skinned grape mutation of Pinot Noir; name reflects the grayish-pink skin color
Italian Pinot Grigio (Veneto, Alto Adige, Friuli): pale gold, light, crisp, neutral — designed for mass-market freshness
Alsatian Pinot Gris (France): full-bodied, spicy, rich, sometimes with noticeable residual sugar — one of Alsace's four noble grapes
Oregon Pinot Gris (USA) typically falls between the two styles in weight and aromatics
Italy is the world's largest producer of Pinot Grigio by volume
The grape originated in Burgundy and is a natural mutation of Pinot Noir; same family as Pinot Blanc and Pinot Noir
Alsace can label wines Pinot Gris regardless of residual sugar, making sweetness level hard to predict from the label

items.prompt: Which hierarchical ranking of Bordeaux châteaux, commissioned by Napoleon III for the 1855 Paris Exposition Universelle, divided the region's top estates into five tiers of 'growths' (crus) based on price and reputation?
items.explanation:
Commissioned by Emperor Napoleon III to showcase France's best wines to visitors at the 1855 Paris World's Fair
Bordeaux wine brokers ranked châteaux in five growths (premier to cinquième cru), based on market price — a proxy for quality
Original First Growths: Châteaux Lafite Rothschild, Latour, Margaux, and Haut-Brion (only non-Médoc estate)
Only one revision in history: 1973, when Mouton Rothschild was elevated from second to first growth after a decades-long campaign by Baron Philippe de Rothschild
The classification covers 61 châteaux total; all are in the Médoc (Left Bank) except Haut-Brion (Graves)
Sauternes (sweet whites) were classified separately in 1855 with Château d'Yquem as the sole 'Premier Cru Supérieur'
Rivals Pomerol and Saint-Émilion have their own, later classification systems; Pétrus is not classified at all
